payload.title: Implement --clone deep-copy for endless-sandbox
payload.description: Phase 1 (E-1074) deferred deep-clone implementation. Per decision E-1071: --clone copies the user's DB plus per-project state (.endless/events/, .endless/plans/, etc.) into the sandbox, and rewrites projects.path rows so events written in the sandbox land in sandboxed locations rather than real project dirs. Cost: ~200-500MB and ~500ms-2s typical. Phase 1 binary accepts the flag but warns and proceeds with empty sandbox; this task implements the actual copy+rewrite.

payload.title: Add 'maybe' phase to task phases
payload.description: Adds a fourth phase 'maybe' for tasks we want to record consideration of without committing to. Distinct from 'later' which implies eventual commitment.

payload.title: Add 'maybe' as a fourth phase alongside now/next/later. Rationale: 'later' implies commitment to do eventually; 'maybe' captures considered-but-undecided so we don't keep relitigating ideas. Promotion path is typically maybe -> now or maybe -> next (skipping later). Pruning of stale 'maybe' items is deferred — they accumulate; a future task will consider periodic pruning.

fields.text:
# E-1088: Add 'maybe' phase to task phases

## Context

Phases today are `now`, `next`, `later`. The user wants a fourth phase `maybe`
to record considered-but-undecided ideas — features we may or may not do, where
the choice should be remembered so we don't keep relitigating it.

`later` implies eventual commitment. `maybe` does not.

## Decision (E-1089)

- Add `maybe` as a fourth phase value alongside `now`, `next`, `later`.
- Promotion path is typically `maybe` → `now` or `maybe` → `next` (skipping
  `later`).
- Convention: when conditional (revisit if X), include a `**Trigger:**` line in
  the plan body that names the trigger.
- Pruning of stale `maybe` items is deferred — they accumulate; a future task
  will consider periodic pruning.

## Scope

The phase value is a free-form string in the events/projector layer (no Go
enum), so changes are concentrated in the Python CLI, the Python heading-map,
and the SQL phase sort orders (Python + Go).

## Changes

### Python — `src/endless/cli.py`

Add `"maybe"` to four `click.Choice(["now", "next", "later"])` lists:

- Line 474 (`task list --phase`)
- Line 550 (`task next --phase`)
- Line 614 (`task search --phase`)
- Line 713 (`task update --phase`)

Update help text on lines 645 and 714 to include `maybe`.

### Python — `src/endless/task_cmd.py`

1. `_phase_for_heading` (line 291): add `"maybe": "maybe"` and a couple of
   plausible aliases (`"considering"`, `"tentative"`).
2. Phase sort `CASE` expression (line 739): extend to
   `WHEN 'now' THEN 0 WHEN 'next' THEN 1 WHEN 'later' THEN 2 WHEN 'maybe' THEN 3 ELSE 4 END`.
3. Same for the `next_tasks` ORDER BY (lines 916–917).

### Go — `internal/web/queries.go`

Extend the phase sort `CASE` expression (lines 180–185) the same way.

### Docs — `docs/guide-2026-04-15-using-endless-in-sessions.md`

Add a `maybe` row to the Task Phases table at line 226.

### Validation that nothing else needs updating

- Go `internal/events/*.go`: phase is a free-form `string`; no enum to extend.
- `templ` templates: only check `item.Phase != "now"`; no enumerated phase
  list, so they keep working.
- Web queries `GetProjectTaskGroups` does not sort by phase.

## Sort-order rationale

`maybe` sorts AFTER `later` (slot 3) because items not committed to should
appear below items that are committed-but-deferred. The `ELSE 4` slot still
catches anything unexpected so future phases don't silently sort first.

## Verification

1. `just build` succeeds (Go + Python).
2. `endless task list --phase maybe` parses (no Click error).
3. `endless task add "X" --phase maybe` creates a task at phase=maybe.
4. `endless task list --sort phase` puts maybe items after later items.
5. `endless task update <id> --phase maybe` round-trips through events.
6. After implementation, add the deferred-pruning meta task at phase=maybe to
   demonstrate the new value works end-to-end.

payload.title: Use 'endless snapshots' command group instead of 'plan-snapshots'
payload.description: Renamed 'endless plan-snapshots' to 'endless snapshots'. Only one snapshot kind exists today (plan snapshots from PostToolUse Write hook). The 'plan-' prefix added namespace overhead with no benefit. If other snapshot kinds are introduced later, they can be subcommands ('snapshots plan list', 'snapshots db list').

payload.title: Implement per-project snapshot commit policy (transparent vs private)
payload.description: Snapshots in .endless/plans/snapshots/ are currently untracked with no .gitignore entry, so they show up in every git status as noise. Some authors will want plans fully transparent (committed); most will want plans private (gitignored). Build a per-project policy: no default, AI prompts on first commit that includes snapshots and persists the choice. Storage location TBD — likely .endless/config.json or a sidecar.

payload.title: No global default for snapshot commit policy because authors differ widely on plan privacy preferences. Forcing one default would be wrong for a large fraction of users. Per-project explicit choice on first commit is the right tradeoff: zero ceremony for greenfield, one prompt for existing projects.
Add AI-evaluated criteria for snapshot exclusion and content redaction
payload.description: Advanced layer on top of E-1091. Author specifies criteria for: (a) which individual snapshots to add to .git/info/exclude (per-snapshot exclusion based on content), (b) what content to redact from snapshot bodies before commit (e.g., secrets, customer names, internal URLs). AI evaluates each snapshot against the author's criteria and applies the policy. Distinct from E-1091's binary transparent/private toggle; this is fine-grained per-snapshot judgment. Depends on E-1091 framework.

payload.title: Redesign snapshot sidecar source_path to a portable shape
payload.description: Replace machine-specific absolute source_path in snapshot sidecars with portable {source_kind, source_name} shape. Detail in attached plan file.

fields.text:
# Decision: portable snapshot sidecar source_path

## Problem

Current sidecar JSON stores `source_path` as a full absolute path:

```json
{ "source_path": "/Users/mikeschinkel/.claude/plans/foo.md" }
```

This is machine-specific, leaks the username, and is meaningless on any other
machine — a hard blocker for committing snapshots cross-developer (E-1091).

## Decision

Replace `source_path` with a portable shape that distinguishes the two cases
`isPlanFile()` matches:

```json
{
  "source_kind": "harness" | "project",
  "source_name": "<basename for harness; project-relative path for project>"
}
```

- For **harness** plans (path under `~/.claude/plans/`), `source_name` is the
  basename (e.g., `e-971-layer-d-hashed-lightning.md`). The `~/.claude/plans/`
  prefix is implied by `source_kind=harness`.
- For **project** plans (path ending in `/plan.md`), `source_name` is the
  path relative to project root (e.g., `docs/plan.md` or `plan.md`).

`source_kind` is explicit even though it could be inferred from whether
`source_name` contains a slash, because explicit is more honest and forward-
compatible (other source kinds may appear).

## Why not just basename + assume `~/.claude/plans/`?

Because `isPlanFile` also matches `*/plan.md` files inside the project. Bare
basename collapses every project plan to `plan.md`, losing which project
subdir it came from.

## Why not the other extreme — full structured location object?

YAGNI. Two source kinds today; both fit cleanly in `{kind, name}`. If a third
kind appears (e.g., a remote plan), add a third enum value.

## Implementation split

- **E-1094**: change writer (`snapshotPlanFile`) and readers (`plan_cmd.py`)
  to emit and consume the new shape. Keep tolerant fallback for
  `source_path` during the transition.
- **E-1095**: migration script to rewrite existing sidecars in
  `.endless/plans/snapshots/` from `source_path` to `source_kind`+`source_name`.
  Run once per project; idempotent.

payload.title: Implement portable source_kind/source_name in snapshot sidecar
payload.description: Per decision E-1094: change snapshotPlanFile writer in cmd/endless-hook/claude.go to emit source_kind+source_name; update plan_cmd.py readers to consume new shape with tolerant fallback to legacy source_path. Out of scope: existing-snapshot migration (E-1095).

payload.title: Migrate existing snapshot sidecars to source_kind/source_name format
payload.description: Per decision E-1094: write a one-shot migration that rewrites every existing snapshot sidecar in .endless/plans/snapshots/ from legacy source_path to source_kind+source_name. Idempotent. Run per project. Blocked by E-1095 (writer must emit new format first).

fields.description: Replace machine-specific absolute source_path in snapshot companion files with portable {source_kind, source_name} shape. Detail in attached plan file.

Per decision E-1094: change snapshotPlanFile writer in cmd/endless-hook/claude.go to emit source_kind+source_name; update plan_cmd.py readers to consume the new shape with tolerant fallback to legacy source_path. Rename internal 'sidecar' references to 'companion' as part of the change. Out of scope: existing-snapshot migration (E-1096).

Per decision E-1094: write a one-shot migration that rewrites every existing snapshot companion JSON in .endless/plans/snapshots/ from legacy source_path to source_kind+source_name. Idempotent. Run per project. Blocked by E-1095 (writer must emit new format first).

payload.title: Add UserPromptSubmit backstop for missed plan snapshots
payload.description: Mirror the BackfillProcess pattern. On UserPromptSubmit (and possibly SessionEnd), enumerate plan files associated with this session and snapshot any whose current content has no matching companion JSON in .endless/plans/snapshots/. Closes the gap from E-994 where a one-shot Write whose snapshot fails has no retry path. Builds on the per-session, content-hash idempotency in existingSnapshot — re-running snapshotPlanFile for already-snapshotted content is already a no-op.

payload.title: Broaden isPlanFile to match PLAN_*.md and *_PLAN.md (case-sensitive uppercase)
payload.description: Current isPlanFile (cmd/endless-hook/claude.go:674) only matches paths under /.claude/plans/ or paths ending in /plan.md (case-insensitive). Per session experience, Claude writes plan-shaped uppercase markdown like PLAN_FOR_FOO.md and FOO_PLAN.md that the hook silently ignores. Add a case-sensitive uppercase regex match: ^(PLAN_.+|.+_PLAN)\.md$ on the basename. Keep existing two clauses. Snapshots for these become valuable iteration archives just like ~/.claude/plans entries.

payload.title: Design hook policy for uppercase markdown files Claude writes (README.md, CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, etc.)
payload.description: Beyond plan files, Claude routinely writes other uppercase-named markdown (README.md, CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, CHANGELOG.md, CONTRIBUTING.md, etc.). Some are project-canonical and authors want them written cleanly; others are agent-introduced noise that should be flagged or blocked. Design a policy: per-project allowlist/blocklist for uppercase .md basenames; default behavior; how to surface flagged writes to the user; whether to block or warn. Distinct from E-1098 (which only widens the plan-snapshot matcher); this is a separate hook concern around editorial quality of agent-authored markdown.

payload.title: Add periodic pruning sweep for stale 'maybe' tasks
payload.description: If 'maybe' tasks accumulate without ever being promoted, a sweep could flag or remove ones older than some threshold. Trigger: revisit if the maybe pile grows past useful size or if scanning becomes friction.

payload.title: Validate phase value at events/projector boundary
payload.description: Phase is enumerated in practice (now/next/later/maybe) but the Go events/projector layer accepts any string. SQL CASEs silently sort unknown values under ELSE, masking bad data. Reject unknown phase values when ingesting events.

payload.title: Re-emit E-1100 phase change via events (currently SQL-only)
payload.description: E-1100's phase=maybe was set via direct SQL UPDATE because the global CLI did not yet accept --phase maybe (E-1088 still on branch e-1088). Once e-1088 lands and 'just install' runs, run: endless task update E-1100 --phase maybe — emits a task.fields_updated event so the change survives a projection rebuild.

payload.title: test in sandbox
test in sandbox
Allow 'sidecar' terminology in Endless prose and code
payload.description: Mike concedes that 'sidecar' is acceptable. Industry usage (XMP photo files, ML metadata pipelines, observability tooling) has long applied the term to passive non-executable companions, eroding the strict K8s 'executable side-process' definition. Preference remains 'companion file' but no enforcement; both terms are valid in Endless context. Memory rule reversed accordingly.

payload.title: Redesign verb-gate error to suppress agent rationalization
payload.description: Current verb-gate error lists 'Common verbs' (invites substitution) and advertises --force (invites bypass). Both train agents to rationalize past the gate. Redesign to: drop alternatives list, drop --force from error text, force binary yes/no on whether the rejected word is a true verb, and possibly require a definition on registration so non-verbs can't be added casually.

fields.text:
# E-1106: Redesign verb-gate error to suppress agent rationalization

## Problem

The current "Title must start with an actionable verb" error trains agents to
rationalize past the gate in two ways:

1. **"Common verbs:" line lists alternatives** (e.g. "add, fix, implement,
   design, …"). Reads as a hint to substitute. For an agent, rewording is the
   path of least resistance — zero extra tool calls, looks like a normal title
   revision. Result: the gate is bypassed without the agent registering its
   actual verb.

2. **"Or use --force to bypass" advertises an escape hatch.** `--force` is for
   humans who know what they're doing. Putting it in every error trains agents
   to use it whenever the rule feels inconvenient.

3. **Three options compete for attention.** "Add a new verb" gets diluted by
   the two non-action options on either side.

Evidence: the agent maintaining this project violated the "always add the verb"
rule twice (2026-04-24 and 2026-05-02) despite having both a memory entry and a
LESSONS.md entry about the rule. Memory rules don't survive contact with
load. The error message has to do the work.

## But: the simple fix opens a worse failure mode

If the new error just says "run `endless phrase add verb '<word>'`", the agent
shifts from "reword the title" to "register the non-verb." A title like
"Maybe rework auth flow" would happily register "maybe" as a verb. The gate's
purpose — catching non-verb titles — gets neutered. Polluted vocabulary is
worse than rephrased titles because it propagates.

So the redesign has to do TWO things:

- Stop nudging toward substitution.
- Stop nudging toward registering non-verbs.

## Proposed error text

```
Error: Title must start with an actionable verb. 'maybe' is not registered.

  Decide: is 'maybe' a true verb? (Does it answer "what action does this perform?")

  IF YES:  endless phrase add verb 'maybe'
  IF NO:   rewrite the title with a verb. Do not register a non-verb to bypass this gate.

  Registering a non-verb defeats the check for everyone — including future-you.
```

Why each line:

- **Drop "Common verbs:" list entirely.** No alternatives = no substitution
  invitation.
- **Drop `--force` from the error.** Move to `--help` only. Humans who need it
  can find it; agents that find it will abuse it.
- **Force a binary** with `IF YES / IF NO`. Agent must mentally commit before
  running the command — not a hand-wavable preamble.
- **Define "verb" with a one-line test** ("what action does this perform?").
  "maybe" fails in two seconds; "consider" passes.
- **Name the social cost** ("defeats the check for everyone"). Stated stakes
  are likelier to be honored than vague rules.

## Stronger lever (optional, recommended)

Require a definition with the registration:

```
endless phrase add verb 'consider' --definition "to deliberate over"
```

Forces the agent to compose semantic justification, which is much harder to
fake than a yes/no. "Maybe" has no plausible "to ___" definition — the agent
either fabricates one (which a reviewer would catch) or rewrites the title.
Mandatory definition turns willful rationalization into a separate falsifiable
claim.

## Optional: agent-aware variant

Claude Code sets identifiable env vars (`CLAUDE_CODE_*`). The error could
detect agent context and show:

- **Humans**: a shorter error without the anti-rationalization preamble.
- **Agents**: the full binary form above.

Different audiences, different failure modes. Humans don't rationalize past
verb gates; agents do. This is a low-risk feature flag — fall back to the
agent-form variant if detection is uncertain.

## Scope

- Update the error text in the title-validation code path
  (`src/endless/task_cmd.py` — search for "actionable verb").
- Decide on definition-required: yes/no. If yes, add `--definition` flag to
  `endless phrase add verb`, persist it in the phrase store, and surface in
  `endless phrase list verb`.
- Decide on agent-aware variant: yes/no. If yes, add detection helper.

## Verification

1. `endless task add "Maybe X"` produces the new error text.
2. The error contains no "Common verbs:" list and no "--force" mention.
3. The error contains the IF YES / IF NO binary.
4. (If --definition required) `endless phrase add verb 'foo'` without
   `--definition` errors with a prompt to provide one.
5. (If agent-aware) the same command from a non-agent shell produces a
   shorter form without the preamble.

## Out of scope

- Audit and prune existing verbs that may have been registered via past
  rationalization. File as a separate "audit verb vocabulary" task if needed.
- Generalizing this pattern to other Endless gates (status, phase, type).
  Each gate has its own failure modes; design per-gate.

payload.title: Set E-1016 phase to 'maybe' once E-1088 lands
payload.description: E-1088 adds the 'maybe' phase. Once that's fully landed (assumed + reinstalled so the CLI accepts it), run: endless task update E-1016 --phase maybe. E-1016 (evaluate session cd deprecation) is open-ended consideration, not committed work — 'maybe' is the right home for it.

fields.prompt:
You are a spawned Claude session working on **E-998** in the `endless` project.

Your goal: when a worktree is created (or via an explicit endless command), generate a `.claude/settings.json` inside that worktree that overrides Claude Code's hook command paths to point at the worktree's own `bin/endless-hook`. This lets a Claude session spawned with cwd inside the worktree exercise the worktree's hook binary without swapping the global symlink and affecting every other live Claude session on the machine.

## Context

- Claude Code's settings precedence: Managed → Command line → Local → Project → User. Project-level `.claude/settings.json` (in the repo root) overrides the user-level `~/.claude/settings.json` for matching keys, including `hooks`.
- The user's `~/.claude/settings.json` registers `endless-hook` (via the symlink at `/usr/local/bin/endless-hook`) for `SessionStart`, `UserPromptSubmit`, `PostToolUse`, `Stop`, `SessionEnd`, etc.
- A worktree-level `.claude/settings.json` that re-registers the same hook events with the path `<worktree>/bin/endless-hook` will cause Claude sessions spawned inside that worktree to invoke the worktree's binary, leaving all other Claude sessions untouched.
- Today, testing hook changes requires `ln -sfn <worktree>/bin/endless-hook /usr/local/bin/endless-hook`, swapping the global. This task replaces that with a per-worktree override.

## What to do

1. `endless task start E-998` to register the session.
2. Read `CLAUDE.md` for project conventions (worktree workflow, install, `just go-work-init` for Go builds).
3. Read `~/.claude/settings.json` to discover which hook events endless registers and what the current invocation looks like. Mirror that structure in the generated worktree settings, but with the path swapped.
4. Implement a generator: probably a `just` recipe (e.g. `just claude-settings-init`) and/or extending whatever creates worktrees today. Keep it idempotent — running twice produces the same file, no churn.
5. The generated file goes at `<worktree-root>/.claude/settings.json`.
6. The path inside the generated file should be `<worktree-root>/bin/endless-hook` (absolute, since the spawned Claude's cwd at hook-fire time is unpredictable).
7. The generated file should be **gitignored** at the worktree level — it's per-developer, references absolute paths, and shouldn't pollute commits. Add `.claude/settings.json` to `.gitignore` if not already there.
8. Verify: from a worktree with the generated settings, spawn Claude (e.g. `endless task spawn <some-task>`), confirm it invokes the worktree's `bin/endless-hook` not the global.

## Constraints / conventions

- Worktree at `.endless/worktrees/e-998/` on branch `e-998-per-worktree-claude-settings`. Standard flow: `git worktree add -b e-998-per-worktree-claude-settings .endless/worktrees/e-998 main && cd .endless/worktrees/e-998 && just go-work-init`.
- Build: `just build`. Tests: `go test ./internal/monitor/... && uv run pytest tests/`.
- The generator must NOT touch the user's `~/.claude/settings.json`. Worktree-only.
- The generator must build the hook binary first (or assume `bin/endless-hook` exists in the worktree). Document either way.
- Be careful: `.claude/settings.json` already exists at the main checkout root. Don't overwrite it. The worktree's `.claude/` is separate filesystem-wise (since worktrees are independent checkouts), so generating a fresh one in the worktree won't collide — but verify.
- If the existing main-checkout `.claude/settings.json` is committed (it probably is, since it controls editor/agent config for the project), make sure your worktree-level .gitignore handles it correctly so generating in the worktree doesn't show as a modification of the committed file.
- Standard commit footer: `Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>`.
- When done: status `verify`, NOT completed.

## Plan file

This is borderline-significant — design + implementation + testing matrix. Strongly consider writing `.endless/plans/E-998.md` capturing:
- What you discovered about the existing hook registration.
- The generator design (recipe, command, both).
- Idempotency strategy.
- Gitignore handling vs the committed main-checkout file.
- Verification: how you confirmed the worktree's hook is exercised and not the global.

If you write a plan file, attach via `endless task update E-998 --text <path>` AND commit it in the same commit (per project rule: the session that creates a plan file owns committing it).

## Status flow when finished

1. Commit on the worktree branch with a clear message referencing E-998.
2. Land: rebase if main has diverged, then `git -C <main> merge --ff-only e-998-per-worktree-claude-settings && git -C <main> worktree remove .endless/worktrees/e-998 && git -C <main> branch -d e-998-per-worktree-claude-settings`.
3. From main: `just install` to refresh.
4. `endless task update E-998 --status verify`.
5. Report what you built, how you verified it, and any open questions you surfaced (file follow-up tasks rather than 'flagging' verbally).

## Out of scope

- Touching the global `~/.claude/settings.json`.
- Changes to the hook code itself.
- A general "extension hook for worktree creation" — that's E-1086, deferred.
- Auto-removal of the worktree settings file when the worktree is dropped (worktree removal already takes the file with it, since it's inside).

payload.title: Audit existing verb vocabulary, backfill definitions, prune non-verbs
payload.description: After E-1106 lands, the verb registry has two pre-existing gaps: (a) every existing verb lacks a definition (registered before --definition was required), and (b) any non-verbs registered via past agent rationalization are still polluting the gate. Walk the verb list, write definitions for legitimate verbs, and remove anything that fails the 'Can a person DO it?' test. Backward-compat code in validate_title can be removed once all verbs have definitions.

payload.title: Add config option for merge-vs-rebase preference
payload.description: When endless adds branch-landing automation (e.g. an 'endless task land' command), the merge-vs-rebase choice must be user-configurable. Trigger: revisit if/when branch-landing automation is designed. Some users have strong opinions either way; tool should respect both.

payload.title: Do not bake a merge-vs-rebase preference into endless. Make it configurable per-project or per-machine. Rationale: developers have strong personal opinions in either direction (some teams mandate rebase, some mandate merge); a tool that picks a side alienates one camp without justification. Default behavior — when no preference is set — should be merge --no-ff, since it is more reversible and matches the worktree-per-task model where each branch is a meaningful unit.

fields.description: When endless adds branch-landing automation (e.g. an 'endless task land' command), the merge-vs-rebase choice must be user-configurable per project or per machine. Some users have strong opinions either way; tool should respect both rather than picking a side.

fields.title: Endless must not bake in a merge-vs-rebase preference. Make it configurable per-project or per-machine. When no preference is set, endless instructs the AI to ASK the user rather than picking a default. Rationale: developers have strong personal opinions in either direction; a tool that silently picks a side alienates one camp. The 'no default' approach surfaces the ambiguity instead of hiding it under a baked-in choice.

payload.title: Fix project-config writes from worktrees writing to wrong .endless/config.json
payload.description: When run from a git worktree, 'endless phrase add' (and any other command writing to project-layer config) writes to the worktree's .endless/config.json instead of the main checkout's. Machine-layer write is correct. Project-layer entries silently diverge between worktree and main, and are lost when the worktree is removed.

payload.title: Project-config writes must always target the main checkout's .endless/config.json regardless of which worktree the command was run from. Use git-common-dir to find the main checkout: parent of git_common_dir is the main worktree's working dir. Falls back to current behavior (walk up from cwd) when not in any git repo.
Design event-commit workflow for non-land merge paths
payload.description: endless worktree land auto-commits .endless/events/*.jsonl as part of landing, but a direct git merge does not. Events accumulate untracked, breaking clone-completeness. Decide whether to auto-commit on every write, periodically, on session end, or wrap git operations.

fields.text:
# E-1113: Design event-commit workflow for non-land merge paths

## Current state

`.endless/events/*.jsonl` files are append-only event logs that **must be
committed to git** (per `docs/guide-2026-04-15-using-endless-in-sessions.md` /
quick-start: "clone-completeness means task state travels with the repo").

Today the only path that commits them automatically is
`endless worktree land`, which auto-commits any dirty paths matching
`AUTO_COMMIT_GLOBS` in `src/endless/worktree_cmd.py:48`:

```python
AUTO_COMMIT_GLOBS = (
    ".endless/events/*.jsonl",
    ".endless/plans/snapshots/*",
    ".endless/config.json",
)
```

The land flow partitions `git status` into auto-commit-files vs user-work-
files, commits the auto-commit set as a separate commit, then proceeds with
the worktree's own commits.

## The gap

Anyone who lands a feature branch via `git merge`, `git rebase`, `git push`,
or any direct git operation (rather than `endless worktree land`) leaves
events files dirty. They accumulate untracked indefinitely.

Concrete observed example: this session merged E-1088, E-1106, E-1111 to main
via `git merge --no-ff`. After all three landed, main still showed:

```
M  .endless/events/events-a7f3-000001.jsonl  (this session's appends)
?? .endless/events/events-1421-000001.jsonl  (this session's new file)
```

A future clone or sync would miss these task-creation events entirely.

## Stakes

- **Clone-completeness**: a fresh clone must reproduce the same DB
  projection. Missing events break this contract.
- **Distributed development**: multi-machine syncs (or future multi-user
  scenarios) depend on events being present in git.
- **Silent failure mode**: nothing warns the developer that events are
  uncommitted. The longer they sit, the more events accumulate, and the
  riskier any "rebase to clean up" maneuver becomes.

## Design options

Each has trade-offs. Pick one or combine.

### A. Auto-commit on every event write

Every `endless task *` / `endless channel *` / etc. that emits an event
also makes a tiny git commit immediately afterwards.

- **Pro**: never loses an event; simplest mental model.
- **Con**: noisy git history (one micro-commit per event); `git log` becomes
  unreadable; commits race with the session's actual work commits; potential
  conflicts during interactive rebase.

### B. Auto-commit periodically (timer / batch)

A daemon or hook commits accumulated event files every N minutes (or N
events).

- **Pro**: less noisy than (A).
- **Con**: requires a long-running process; still produces a separate event
  commit stream that interleaves with feature commits; bounding latency vs.
  noise is a tuning problem with no good answer.

### C. Auto-commit at session end

When a Claude Code session ends (or `endless task verify` is run, or a hook
fires on shell exit), commit accumulated events.

- **Pro**: feels natural — events are committed when the session that
  created them concludes.
- **Con**: detecting "session end" reliably is hard (Ctrl-C, crash, kill,
  laptop sleep). Without reliable detection, this is just (B) by another name.

### D. Wrap git operations on the main checkout

Hook into `git merge`, `git rebase`, `git push` (via git hooks or a
shell wrapper) so they auto-commit events the same way `endless worktree
land` does.

- **Pro**: aligns merge path with land path; events get committed at the
  natural integration boundary.
- **Con**: shell hooks are easy to bypass; git hooks live in `.git/hooks`
  which isn't committed (each clone needs setup); pre-merge hook timing
  is fiddly.

### E. Block direct git merge / rebase on main, force land path

`endless-hook` already blocks direct `git commit` on main's working tree
(see `cmd/endless-hook/claude.go:347` — comment references E-1012). Extend
to block direct `git merge` / `git rebase` on main, with an error that
points at `endless worktree land`.

- **Pro**: collapses the two paths into one — there is no "non-land merge"
  to handle.
- **Con**: aggressive; locks users out of standard git workflow; only
  enforceable for sessions running under endless-hook.

### F. Surface uncommitted events as a warning, not a fix

Add a status check (`endless status --check-events` or a pre-commit hook)
that warns when events are dirty. Don't auto-commit; just shout.

- **Pro**: zero behavior change; informational only.
- **Con**: depends on user noticing the warning; the bug-prone manual step
  remains.

## Open questions for the events-log session

1. Which option (or combination) fits the project's clone-completeness
   contract best?
2. Is there a reason `endless worktree land` is the canonical merge path
   that I'm missing? (If yes, option E becomes more attractive — make the
   canonical path the only path.)
3. How should this interact with the multi-session concurrency model? Each
   session writes to its own `events-{node}-{seq}.jsonl`, so file-level
   conflicts between sessions are unlikely, but the auto-commit step is a
   shared write to git's index.
4. Backward compatibility: there are already existing dirty event files in
   developer checkouts (this session left some). Migration story?

## Out of scope

- Implementation of the chosen option. This task captures the decision; a
  follow-up implementation task should be filed once the design is settled.
- Changing the existing `endless worktree land` auto-commit behavior. It
  works; new design should be additive.

payload.title: Isolate endless-sandbox enter subshell in its own process group
payload.description: Today, processes started inside an enter subshell (e.g. .zshrc daemons, prompt hooks) can outlive the shell and keep writing to the sandbox path after the user exits. destroy then can't keep up with the leaked writers, leaving stale dirs that block re-entry. Discovered during E-1074 verify on 2026-05-02: a leaked enter from 02:52AM kept recreating files in ~/.cache/endless/sandboxes/probe/ for hours, surviving multiple destroys. Fix: setpgid the subshell, signal the whole group on enter exit and on signal handler. Also consider blocking destroy when the sandbox has live writers, with a clear error pointing at the offender.

payload.title: Fix endless session use sibling-pane lookup in same tmux window
payload.description: On 2026-05-02, 'eval "$(endless session use)"' from a tmux pane in the same window as the Claude Code pane reported 'No sibling Claude pane in this tmux window. Run `endless session list` to see all candidates project-wide.' The sibling Claude pane was demonstrably running in the same window. Investigate the pane-discovery logic: tmux query, env-var matching (TMUX_PANE), or the heuristic for what counts as a 'Claude pane'. Likely related to how Claude's process tree or env is identified across sessions.

payload.title: Fix Python db._migrate to tolerate empty DB (no tasks table)
payload.description: src/endless/db.py:_migrate runs ALTER TABLE on tasks without checking existence; an empty DB raises sqlite3.OperationalError: no such table: tasks. Surfaces inside E-1074 sandbox shells (and on fresh installs) when a Go binary creates the DB file before Python's is_new check. Will block E-1078 pytest fixture on every fresh sandbox.

fields.prompt:
# E-894 — Move task display reads from Python to Go

You are starting a planning session for E-894. Begin with `endless task show E-894` to confirm the task state.

## Context

After E-805 moved task *writes* to Go via the events-authoritative pipeline (`cmd/endless-event/main.go`), Python still queries SQLite directly for task display — `task list`, `task show`, `task next`, `task search`. Per Mike's house rule (memory `feedback_no_python_sqlite_reads`), no new Python SQLite reads should be written; legacy reads should migrate. This task is `needs_plan` — your deliverable is the plan, not implementation.

## Why now

E-1116 just surfaced a concrete consequence of the split DB-ownership status quo: Python's `src/endless/db.py:_migrate` ALTERs `tasks` without checking the table exists, and crashes with `sqlite3.OperationalError: no such table: tasks` on a truly empty DB. Empty DBs were impossible historically because something always ran first; under E-1074 sandboxes (E-1078 fixture in particular) they become the norm. The fragmentation E-894 was filed to address is now actively breaking new work. Your plan must take a position on scope:

- **(a) reads only** — schema ownership stays split (Python's `schema.sql` creates `tasks`; Go's `monitor.DB()` migrates `sessions`/`channels`/etc.). E-1116 patches the empty-DB crash; E-894 narrowly moves the four read commands.
- **(b) reads + schema ownership** — Python becomes a thin shell-out client; Go owns DB init, schema, migrations entirely. E-1116 becomes subsumed.

Mike's prior signals lean (b), but read the relevant code before deciding — cost curves matter.

## Files to read first (use the Explore agent in parallel, ≤3 agents)

1. `src/endless/task_cmd.py` — current Python task-read commands
2. `src/endless/db.py` — Python's SQLite wrapper: `get_db()`, `_init_schema`, `_migrate`, `query()`
3. `cmd/endless-event/main.go` — existing Go subcommand-CLI pattern (mirror this for any new binary)
4. `cmd/endless-sandbox/main.go` — recent example of the same pattern (E-1074)
5. `internal/monitor/db.go` — Go DB layer: `ConfigDir()`, `CacheDir()`, `DB()`, migrate functions
6. `internal/schema/schema.sql` — canonical schema; today only Python runs it
7. `internal/events/` — events-authoritative read helpers; understand what's already query-able from Go

## Constraints

- **No new Python SQLite code.** New Python code shells out to Go binaries. Legacy paths only stay where unavoidable.
- **Events-authoritative principle preserved.** Reads project from events; do not introduce write paths that bypass the event log.
- **JSON-line output from Go binaries** for easy Python parsing, matching `endless-event`'s convention.
- **No backwards-compat shims** (memory `feedback_no_legacy`). When a path moves, the old path is deleted.
- Apply Mike's Go house conventions (ClearPath, doterr, go-dt, go-cliutil) — invoke the relevant skills (`go-clearpath-auditor`, `go-error-handling-author`, `go-cli-tui-author`) when designing API shape.

## Deliverable

Write the plan to `.endless/plans/E-894.md` per project convention. Required sections:

- **Context** — why now, what E-1116 revealed, the cost of staying split
- **Decision: scope** — reads-only vs reads+schema-ownership, with rationale
- **Architecture** — new Go binary or extend `endless-event`? CLI surface? JSON output shape? How does Python's `task_cmd.py` invoke it?
- **Implementation phases** — split into child tasks; file via `endless task add --parent E-894` after Mike approves
- **Files to create / modify** — concrete paths
- **Reusable existing helpers** — extend what's there rather than reinvent
- **Verification plan** — how each phase is proven
- **Out of scope** — what stays Python for now (if anything) and why

After writing, attach via `endless task update E-894 --text .endless/plans/E-894.md` (this also flips status `needs_plan` → `ready` per memory `feedback_plan_attach_promotes_to_ready`). Then call `ExitPlanMode` for Mike's review.

## Worktree setup (manual, before any exploration)

```bash
git -C /Users/mikeschinkel/Projects/endless worktree add -b task/894-task-reads-go \
    /Users/mikeschinkel/Projects/endless/.endless/worktrees/e-894 main
cd /Users/mikeschinkel/Projects/endless/.endless/worktrees/e-894
just go-work-init
endless task start E-894
```

Do NOT run `just install` from the worktree (memory `feedback_no_just_install_from_worktree`).
Do NOT bundle E-1116 implementation work into this worktree (memory `feedback_one_task_per_worktree`) — your plan can subsume it, but the fix lands in its own task/branch.

## Boundaries

- Planning session. **No implementation code** (no Go, no Python edits) until Mike approves the plan.
- Use the Explore agent for code reading. Use the Plan agent if you want a second opinion on plan structure before writing.
- The plan file is the deliverable. Child tasks for implementation phases are filed only after plan approval.

## Things Mike has emphasized in prior planning conversations

- Robustness over a few ms of speed
- Visibility over temporary annoyance
- File don't flag — surface a follow-up, run `endless task add` (memory `feedback_flagging_means_filing`)
- Move fast in bootstrapping; ship over process (memory `feedback_move_fast`)
- Discuss before updating plan decisions (memory `feedback_discuss_before_updating`)

payload.title: Redesign verb storage: extract verbs from matchers into top-level array
payload.description: Verbs are domain-special: they carry definitions (and potentially aliases, examples) that other matcher types do not. Stuffing 'definitions' as a sibling field on the generic matcher object coupled type-specific content into the schema. Move verbs to a top-level 'verbs: [{value, definition}]' array with a bespoke 'endless verb' command group; let other matcher types stay generic under 'matchers'. Shared matching engine stays shared.

fields.text:
# E-1117: Redesign verb storage — top-level verbs array, decoupled from matchers

## Background

E-1106 introduced `--definition` on `endless phrase add verb` (good: forces
semantic justification). E-1108 backfilled definitions for the existing 79
verbs. Both stored definitions as a sibling `definitions: {value: def}` map on
the verb-type matcher object.

That coupled verb-specific content into the generic matcher schema. The
schema started saying "any matcher might have definitions" when only verbs
ever do — pivots, regex command-patterns, channel matchers carry vestigial
nulls. Discussion in this session converged on a cleaner shape.

## Considered and rejected

**Option C — uniform per-value `meta` array on every matcher's `match`
list:**

```json
"match": [{"value": "broaden", "meta": [{"name": "definition", "value": "..."}]}]
```

Restores schema purity at the cost of CLI ergonomics (`endless phrase add
broaden --type verb --meta "definition:..."`), validation strength (definition
is a stringly-typed bag entry, not a typed field), and discoverability (domain
knowledge that verbs have definitions is hidden). Bad trade for verbs given
how load-bearing definitions are.

**Option B (original) — top-level verbs as a map keyed by value:**

```json
"verbs": {"broaden": {"definition": "..."}}
```

Map-keyed-by-value is a Go pain (lookup easy; ordering nondeterministic;
adding fields like `added_at` later is more verbose to migrate than slice
handling). Rejected in favor of array-of-objects.

## Chosen design

```json
{
  "verbs": [
    {"value": "broaden", "definition": "to widen scope or coverage"},
    {"value": "consider", "definition": "to deliberate over"}
  ],
  "matchers": [
    {"type": "pivot", "method": "substring", "match": ["actually", "wait"]},
    {"type": "start", "scope": "task", "method": "regex", "match": "..."}
  ]
}
```

- Verbs are a top-level `verbs` array of objects with `value` and `definition`
  required, future fields optional.
- Matchers no longer contain `type=verb` entries. Stay generic for pivots,
  regex command-patterns, channel matchers.
- The match engine (exact / substring / regex detection) stays a pure shared
  function. Verb-gate calls it with patterns drawn from `verbs[*].value`;
  phrase consumers call it with patterns from `matchers`. Same engine,
  different data sources.

## Why bespoke for verbs is justified

Verbs have rich domain content (definitions today; aliases, examples,
synonyms plausible later) that is load-bearing for verb-gate correctness.
Other matcher types currently do not. If pivots later grow rich metadata,
extract them the same way — pattern-following, not novel design. YAGNI now.

## CLI surface

```
endless verb add <value> --definition "<def>"
endless verb list [--json]
endless verb remove <value>
```

`endless phrase add verb` is removed (no compat shim — endless is "paving
cowpaths"). Future commands like `endless verb show <value>` or `--alias`
land naturally.

## Implementation

### Files

- New: `src/endless/verb_cmd.py` — CRUD for the `verbs` array.
- New: `src/endless/cli.py` — `verb` command group; remove the `verb` special
  case in the existing `phrase add` handler.
- Modified: `src/endless/matchers.py` — `get_verbs()` reads from `verbs`
  top-level array. Drop `type=verb` from `add_match_value`. One-time
  migration helper that runs at config load: extract any existing
  `type=verb` matchers into `verbs`, drop the `definitions` map.
- Modified: `src/endless/phrase_cmd.py` — remove `definition` parameter and
  the verb-required-definition check (verb code path is gone from phrase).
- Modified: `src/endless/task_cmd.py` — `validate_title` is unchanged in
  shape (still calls `matchers.get_verbs()`); only the storage backend
  underneath changed.

### Tests

- `tests/test_verb_cmd.py` — add/list/remove, definition required, persists
  in `verbs` array, layered config (project + machine).
- `tests/test_verb_gate.py` — update existing tests to reflect new storage
  (verbs come from top-level array, not matcher list).
- Migration test: a config with old-shape `type=verb` matchers (with or
  without `definitions`) is auto-migrated on first load.

### Migration

One-pass, idempotent, runs at config load:

1. If `matchers` contains any `type=verb` entries, extract their `match`
   list values into `verbs[]`. If a `definitions` map is present on the
   verb matcher, hoist values into the per-verb `definition` field.
2. Remove the verb matchers from `matchers`.
3. Save back.

Idempotent because step 1 finds nothing to do once migration is complete.

## Cleanup of prior bad work

- Revert `fb60208` (the E-1108 commit that added the bad `definitions`
  structure to project config).
- Machine config `definitions` field is removed automatically by the new
  migration on first load.

## Out of scope

- Migrating other matcher types (pivot, etc.) into bespoke top-level
  structures. Defer until they have metadata that warrants the work.
- Adding alias / example / synonym fields to verbs. Schema is forward-
  compatible (add fields as needed); no implementation today.
- Backward-compat shim that keeps `endless phrase add verb` working.
  Removing it cleanly is fine — endless is in active development.

## Verification

1. `just build` clean.
2. Full Python test suite passes.
3. `endless verb add ponder --definition "to deliberate"` writes a new
   `{value: "ponder", definition: "to deliberate"}` entry in the project
   `verbs` array.
4. `endless verb list` shows all verbs with definitions.
5. `endless task add "Ponder X"` accepts (verb-gate reads new storage).
6. `endless task add "Maybe X"` rejects with the E-1106 anti-rationalization
   error message.
7. Reading a config that still has old-shape `type=verb` matchers triggers
   migration; the file ends up in the new shape after first read.

# E-894 — Move task display reads from Python to Go

## Context

After E-805 moved task **writes** to Go via the events-authoritative pipeline, four task-display commands still query SQLite directly from Python: `task list`, `task show`, `task next`, `task search` — plus secondary read paths in `active_tasks`, `recent_tasks`, `list_decisions`, relation queries (`get_all_relations`, `_related_task_ids`, `show_relations`), `_project_root_for_task`, and a single `tasks` SELECT in `session_cmd.py:1300`. Per house rule (memory `feedback_no_python_sqlite_reads`), no new Python SQLite reads are written; legacy reads should migrate.

E-1116 surfaced a concrete cost of the split status quo: Python's `db.py:_migrate` ALTERs `tasks` without checking the table exists, crashing on a truly empty DB. Empty DBs were impossible historically, but under E-1074 sandboxes (E-1078 fixture in particular) they become routine. The exploration also confirmed a deeper divergence: **Python's migration chain runs through V6 (V5 task_deps active-voice rebuild + V6 `outcome` column); Go's runs only through V4**. Whichever side creates the DB first determines whether V5/V6 land. The split DB-ownership model is not just stylistically inconsistent — it has bug surface.

E-1053 already established the pilot for the read-side migration: `focus_cmd.py` reads via `query_bridge.py` shelling out to `endless-event` Go subcommands (`get-focus`, `get-session`, `project-by-path`, `focus-ancestors`, `focus-subtree`), with read helpers in `internal/events/query.go`. E-894 mirrors that pattern for tasks.

## Decision: Scope

**Option B — reads + schema ownership, in phases.**

Phases 1-2 deliver the read migration (E-894's nominal scope). Phases 3-4 move schema/migration ownership into Go (subsumes E-1116). Phase 5 deletes Python's SQLite footprint entirely.

Rationale:
- Option A (reads only) leaves the fragility E-1116 surfaced. The two Python-only migrations (V5 task_deps active-voice, V6 `outcome` column) remain Python's responsibility, contradicting E-894's direction "Python has zero SQLite knowledge for task operations".
- Costs (rough): Option A ~4-6 hr; Option B adds ~7-10 hr for full schema ownership. The full ownership is a one-time cost; the alternative is paying schema-divergence tax indefinitely.
- E-1116's narrow fix is "patch Python's `_migrate` to tolerate empty DB". The structural fix is "stop running Python migrations at all" — and E-894 already does most of the work to get there.
- Mike's prior signals (memories: `feedback_no_python_sqlite_reads`, `feedback_no_legacy`, `feedback_decouple_before_ship`) favor full decoupling over partial.

Phasing lets each phase land independently. If Mike wants to stop after Phase 2, the read migration is complete and useful on its own; Phases 3-5 can still happen later without rework.

## Architecture

**Extend `cmd/endless-event/`** rather than create a new binary. Precedent: `get-focus`, `focus-ancestors`, etc. are already there per E-1053. Adding task subcommands keeps the Python bridge layer's binary discovery (`shutil.which("endless-event")`) unchanged.

**Read helpers** go in `internal/events/query.go` alongside the focus helpers. Each helper returns a typed struct with JSON tags; the main.go runner marshals to stdout.

**Python bridge** extends the existing `src/endless/query_bridge.py` with task functions (mirrors how `get_focus`, `get_session` already live there). If `query_bridge.py` grows unwieldy, split into `query_bridge_focus.py` / `query_bridge_task.py` at implementation time — judgment call, default is to extend.

**JSON output convention** (mirrors focus pilot exactly):
- Single-row queries: one JSON object on stdout, or literal `null` if not found.
- List queries: JSON array on stdout, or `[]` if empty.
- Errors: stderr message prefixed `endless-event: error:`, exit 1 (or exit 2 for missing required flags).

**Subcommand surface (Phase 1):**

| Subcommand                       | Replaces                                          | Output      |
|----------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|-------------|
| `get-task --id N`                | `task show` core query (`detail_item`)            | object/null |
| `list-tasks [filters]`           | `task list` core query (`show_plan`)              | array       |
| `next-tasks [filters --limit N]` | `task next` core query (`next_tasks`)             | array       |
| `search-tasks --query S [...]`   | `task search` core query (`search_tasks`)         | array       |
| `task-children --id N`           | `detail_item --children` query                    | array       |
| `task-relations --id N [--rel-type T]` | `show_relations`, `_related_task_ids`       | array       |
| `active-tasks`                   | `active_tasks` (in_progress + verify)             | array       |
| `recent-tasks [--limit N]`       | `recent_tasks`                                    | array       |
| `list-decisions [filters]`       | `list_decisions` (type='decision')                | array       |
| `task-by-source-file --path P`   | `_project_root_for_task` SQL                      | object/null |

Filters mirror the existing Click flags: `--phase`, `--status`, `--tier`, `--parent`, `--related-to`, `--rel-type`, `--all`, `--sort`, `--limit`. Flag syntax follows pilot precedent (raw `flag.FlagSet`).

Output JSON shapes must preserve every field Python's `--json`/`--llm` formatters consume today (full set captured per command in the agent inventory). Python continues to render Rich tables, markdown, and tree views — only the data-fetch step moves.

**Conventions (house rules):** new Go code applies ClearPath (`go-clearpath-auditor`), doterr (`go-error-handling-author`), and go-dt for paths. The pilot pre-dates strict adherence in some places; new code is held to the house standard. Migrating the pilot itself to house conventions is out of scope (separate task).

## Implementation phases

Each phase becomes a child task filed via `endless task add --parent E-894` after plan approval. Phases gate on the prior phase landing on main; each gets its own worktree per `feedback_one_task_per_worktree`.

**Phase 1 — Go read helpers + subcommands**
- Add task structs (`TaskRow`, `TaskChildRow`, `TaskRelationRow`, etc.) and helper functions to `internal/events/query.go`.
- Wire each helper to a runner in `cmd/endless-event/main.go` (or split runners into per-file modules following sandbox precedent if main.go grows too large).
- Reuse SQL from `internal/web/queries.go` where it already exists for tasks (DRY); lift shared queries into `query.go` rather than duplicate.
- Unit tests for each helper against a fixture DB.

**Phase 2 — Python bridge + cutover**
- Extend `src/endless/query_bridge.py` with `get_task`, `list_tasks`, `next_tasks`, `search_tasks`, `task_children`, `task_relations`, `active_tasks`, `recent_tasks`, `list_decisions`, `task_by_source_file`.
- Replace every `db.query(...)` call against the `tasks` / `task_deps` tables in `task_cmd.py`, `session_cmd.py`, and any other read-side caller with the bridge.
- Delete Python's read-side SQL bodies (`active_tasks`, `recent_tasks`, `list_decisions`, `get_all_relations`, `_related_task_ids`, `_project_root_for_task` SQL, `detail_item` SQL, `show_plan` SQL, `next_tasks` SQL, `search_tasks` SQL — keep formatting code, drop SQL).
- Run all tests; smoke-test each CLI command against golden output.

**Phase 3 — Move V5 + V6 migrations to Go**
- Port `_migrate_v5` (task_deps active-voice rebuild) into `migrateV5` in `internal/monitor/db.go`. Largest piece — disables FKs, rebuilds the table, swaps source/target on subset of rows. Validate with `endless-event validate-db` against pre-V5 DB snapshots.
- Port `_migrate_v6` (add `outcome` column to tasks) into `migrateV6` (trivial ALTER).
- Wire both into the existing `migrate()` orchestrator at `internal/monitor/db.go:140`.

**Phase 4 — Go owns DB initialization (subsumes E-1116)**
- Verify `monitor.DB()` is the only DB-creation path. Audit Python for any remaining `_init_schema`/`_migrate` invocations and remove them.
- Subsumes E-1116: Python no longer touches the DB at all on startup. Mark E-1116 `assumed` once Phase 4 lands. The empty-DB crash disappears because Python no longer runs ALTERs.

**Phase 5 — Delete Python db.py reads/migrations**
- Remove `_init_schema`, `_migrate*`, `query`, `scalar`, `exists` from `src/endless/db.py`. Delete `db.py` entirely if nothing else imports it; otherwise reduce to a stub.
- Remove `import sqlite3` from any Python module that still has it.
- Final sweep: grep `src/endless/` for `db.query(`, `sqlite3`, `.execute(`. Should be zero outside test fixtures.

## Files to create / modify

**Modify:**
- `internal/events/query.go` — add task helpers (after existing focus helpers)
- `cmd/endless-event/main.go` — register new subcommands; consider per-command files (see sandbox precedent) if file grows
- `internal/monitor/db.go` — Phase 3: add `migrateV5`, `migrateV6`; orchestrator wiring
- `src/endless/query_bridge.py` — Phase 2: extend with task functions
- `src/endless/task_cmd.py` — Phase 2: replace SQL with bridge calls in `show_plan`, `detail_item`, `next_tasks`, `search_tasks`, `active_tasks`, `recent_tasks`, `list_decisions`, `get_all_relations`, `_related_task_ids`, `show_relations`, `_project_root_for_task`
- `src/endless/session_cmd.py:1300-1302` — Phase 2: replace SELECT with `query_bridge.get_task`
- `src/endless/db.py` — Phase 5: delete or reduce to stub

**Confirm-only (no change needed):**
- `internal/schema/schema.sql` — `outcome` column already at line 91; canonical schema is correct

**Create (optional):**
- `cmd/endless-event/task_reads.go` (or per-command files) if `main.go` becomes too large

## Reusable existing helpers

- `internal/events/query.go:GetFocus` (`internal/events/query.go:55`) — single-row pattern with nullable handling; copy for `GetTask`.
- `internal/events/query.go:FocusAncestors`, `FocusSubtree` — list/tree patterns; reuse for `TaskChildren`, `TaskRelations`.
- `internal/events/query.go:FocusRow`, `FocusTreeNode` — struct conventions with JSON tags + `omitempty` for nullable FKs.
- `cmd/endless-event/main.go:runGetFocus` (lines 49-68) — runner template; copy for each new subcommand.
- `src/endless/query_bridge.py:_run_query` (lines 14-40) — subprocess + JSON parsing; reuse as-is.
- `src/endless/query_bridge.py:get_focus` (etc.) — Python wrapper template.
- `internal/web/queries.go` — Go-side task SQL already exists for the web dashboard. Lift shared queries into `internal/events/query.go` rather than rewrite, then have web call the same helpers (DRY).
- `internal/events/projector.go:replayEvent`, `internal/events/executor.go` — reference for how task fields land in SQLite. No changes needed; reads project from current persisted state.

## Verification

**Phase 1:**
- `go test ./internal/events/...` passes for new helpers (per-helper table-driven tests).
- `endless-event get-task --id 894` returns valid JSON object matching the agent-inventory shape.
- `endless-event list-tasks --status ready --phase now --sort tier` returns expected rows in expected order.
- Each new subcommand exits 0 on success/empty, 1 on DB error, 2 on missing required flag.
- `endless-event validate-db` still passes.

**Phase 2:**
- `endless task list`, `task show E-894`, `task next`, `task search "migrate"`, `task active`, `task recent`, `task decisions` produce identical output to pre-migration (golden-file diff with same DB snapshot).
- All output formats preserved: default Rich table, `--json`, `--llm`, `--tree`, `--no-description`, `--text`, `--prompt`, `--children`, `--outcome`.
- All filter combinations exercised: `--status`, `--phase`, `--tier`, `--parent`, `--related-to`, `--rel-type`, `--all`, `--sort`.
- `pytest` passes (`just test`).
- Grep `src/endless/` for `db.query(.*tasks` — zero hits.

**Phase 3:**
- `endless-event validate-db` against a pre-V5 DB rebuilds correctly (active-voice task_deps + `outcome` column present).
- Test against three fixtures: a fresh DB, a pre-V5 DB snapshot (use a backup from `~/.config/endless/backups/`), and a fully-migrated DB.
- No FK constraint violations during V5 rebuild.

**Phase 4:**
- Fresh sandbox (E-1078 fixture) creates DB via Go alone; no Python init code runs. E-1116's `OperationalError: no such table: tasks` no longer reproduces.
- `pytest` passes against an empty `~/.config/endless/` directory.

**Phase 5:**
- `grep -r 'import sqlite3' src/endless/` — zero hits.
- `grep -r 'from .db' src/endless/` — zero hits (or only stub references).
- Full smoke: `just install`, fresh `~/.config/endless/`, run end-to-end CLI workflow including `task add`, `task show`, `task list`, `task next`, `task search`, `task complete`, `session start`.

## Out of scope

- **Migrating the focus pilot to house conventions (go-cliutil, etc.)** — pilot uses raw `flag.FlagSet`. New code follows the same precedent for symmetry. A separate cleanup task can upgrade the entire `endless-event` surface to go-cliutil.
- **Web dashboard reads** (`internal/web/queries.go`) — already in Go; not part of E-894. Phase 1 lifts shared SQL into `query.go` so web reuses it (DRY), but the web UI cutover is unchanged.
- **TUI reads** — already Go, untouched.
- **Other tables' migrations** — E-894 takes ownership of `tasks` + `task_deps` migration history (V5+V6) because those are what's behind. Other tables (sessions, channels, etc.) already match between Go and Python; no shift needed. A future task can sweep the rest if Mike wants strict "Go owns all schema".
- **E-1116 narrow fix** — superseded by Phase 4. Don't separately patch Python's `_migrate`; just remove it.

payload.title: Fix endless task relations to not invert replaces/replaced_by labels
payload.description: endless task relations TASK_ID renders the 'replaces' relation under a 'Replaced by:' header (and presumably the inverse). endless task deps TASK_ID renders the same data correctly under 'Replaces:'. Likely an inverse-direction label mixup in the relations grouping. Reproduced today: ran 'task replace E-1116 --by E-894 --status obsolete'; 'task deps E-894' showed 'Replaces: E-1116' (correct); 'task relations E-894' showed 'Replaced by: E-1116' (inverted).

payload.title: Add Foo
Consider adding phase=not-applicable or none when setting status to a terminal: obsolete, confirmed, etc.
Change 'task update' Updated message to show '<label>: <old_value>-><new_value>'
Apply click.Choice to task add --phase to match other --phase commands
payload.description: task add's --phase flag accepts any string at parse time because it has no click.Choice type; task list / next / search / update all have click.Choice(['now','next','later','maybe']). The asymmetry means bad values reach the events layer before being rejected (E-1101 catches them, but with a worse error). One-line fix: add the type to task add's --phase decorator.

payload.title: Migrate all machine-layer verbs into endless project config
payload.description: Verbs accumulated in the machine layer during endless work were derived from this project and should travel with the repo. Copy all verb entries from ~/.config/endless/config.json into <repo>/.endless/config.json, deduplicated by value. Machine layer left intact so other projects on this machine still have access via the merge.

payload.title: Define branch-collision strategy for short-id session worktrees
payload.description: If Layer F creates session-flavor worktrees using a short-id prefix of the session UUID (e.g. first 8 chars), branch name collisions are theoretically possible across sessions sharing that prefix. Decide: retry with longer prefix? Use full UUID? Random suffix? Layer D itself doesn't create worktrees so this is purely a Layer F concern; recording it as a maybe so it isn't forgotten.

payload.title: Extract verbs from config.json into separate .endless/verbs.json file
payload.description: After E-1117 + E-1122, verbs dominate the config file (80+ entries) and obscure the config-oriented fields (roots, ignore, ownership, etc.). Move verbs out into their own file at .endless/verbs.json (project) and ~/.config/endless/verbs.json (machine). Top-level JSON array of {value, definition} objects. Migration extracts existing 'verbs' key from config.json on first load. Verb-gate validation reads from new path.

fields.title: Fix _migrate_v5 non-idempotent swap UPDATEs flipping blocks/replaces rows on every connect
fields.description: src/endless/db.py:_migrate_v5 contains two UPDATE statements that swap source/target on rows where dep_type IN ('needs','blocks') and where dep_type='replaces'. They have no version gate, so they run on every endless command and flip every blocks/replaces row's direction each time. Result: data corruption — at any moment, ~half of rows are in their intended direction and ~half are inverted, depending on parity of past invocations. Symptoms originally observed as inconsistent labels in 'task show' / 'task relations' / 'task detail' for the same task; the labels were correct, the underlying data was flapping. Fix: gate the swap UPDATEs on PRAGMA user_version; set user_version=5 once V5 has run. Audit existing data afterwards (5 replaces rows + 60 blocks rows). Pre-existing data corruption from past flips was repaired manually post-patch. E-863 (proper schema version migration system) is the structural fix that would have prevented this.

payload.title: Add CLI commands for tmux window/task lineage introspection
payload.description: To support a second tmux status line that shows window/task relationships, we need CLI primitives that expose: (1) which tmux window spawned the current one (parent window discovery), (2) the tree of tasks the current window has worked on, (3) how the current window's tasks relate to tasks in sibling windows. The tmux integration itself is separate; this task is just the data-side CLI surface so the status-line script has something to call. Likely shape: 'endless session lineage', 'endless session tasks', 'endless session siblings' — names TBD.

fields.text:
# E-863 — Implement schema version migration system

## Context

E-1118 just demonstrated the cost of having no schema version system: Python's `_migrate_v5` ran on every connect with no version guard, flipping every `blocks`/`replaces` row's direction. The patch used `PRAGMA user_version` as an ad-hoc gate. Generalize that pattern across all migrations so future migrations can't repeat the same bug.

Today's state:
- **Two migration runners**: Go (`internal/monitor/db.go:migrate` runs V1-V4) and Python (`src/endless/db.py:_migrate` runs V1-V6) both run on every DB connection. They overlap but aren't synchronized — Python is two versions ahead.
- **No version gate** on most migration steps; idempotency is ad-hoc (some via `IF NOT EXISTS`, some via guards, some absent — the V5 case).
- **No explicit `migrate` command**; migrations run as a side effect of opening the DB.
- **No "rebuild requires opt-in"** rule; V5's table rebuild ran whenever the schema looked off.

## Decision: scope (minimal — framework only)

**E-863 establishes the framework. It does NOT port Python's V5/V6 to Go.** That port lives in E-894 Phase 3, which will be the first user of E-863's framework.

Rationale: tier 2 (quick) sized E-863 for ~half a session. Adding V5/V6 port (with V5's tricky table rebuild) would push it to tier 3+ and entangle two unrelated concerns. After E-863 lands, V5/V6 ports become straightforward registry additions.

## Decision: version store

**Use `PRAGMA user_version` (built-in SQLite integer).** E-863's description proposed a `_schema_version` table; the pragma is simpler and already used by the E-1118 fix. Pros:
- Atomic write, no extra table, standard SQLite practice.
- Already in active use (set to 5 by E-1118 patch).
- Inspectable via `sqlite3 db "PRAGMA user_version"`.

Cons: no audit history (can't see "when was V3 applied"). For Endless's needs that's fine — backups serve as point-in-time history.

Flip to `_schema_version` table if you want history; mechanics translate trivially.

## Architecture

**Go orchestrator** in `internal/monitor/db.go`:

```go
const CurrentSchemaVersion = 4  // bump when adding a migration

type Migration struct {
    Version       int
    Name          string
    Apply         func(*sql.DB) error
    RequiresRebuild bool  // true → only runs with explicit --force-rebuild
}

var migrations = []Migration{
    {Version: 1, Name: "legacy plan→task + base tables",   Apply: migrateV1},
    {Version: 2, Name: "drop dead tables, rename, tier",   Apply: migrateV2},
    {Version: 3, Name: "session conversation history",     Apply: migrateV3},
    {Version: 4, Name: "task_files, suggestions",          Apply: migrateV4},
}

func migrate(db *sql.DB, opts MigrateOpts) error {
    cur, _ := userVersion(db)
    if cur >= CurrentSchemaVersion {
        return nil  // up to date, no work, no backup
    }
    if !opts.SkipBackup {
        BackupDB()
    }
    for _, m := range migrations {
        if m.Version <= cur {
            continue
        }
        if m.RequiresRebuild && !opts.AllowRebuild {
            return fmt.Errorf("migration v%d requires table rebuild; rerun `endless db migrate --force-rebuild`", m.Version)
        }
        if err := m.Apply(db); err != nil {
            return fmt.Errorf("migration v%d (%s): %w", m.Version, m.Name, err)
        }
        if _, err := db.Exec(fmt.Sprintf("PRAGMA user_version = %d", m.Version)); err != nil {
            return err
        }
    }
    return nil
}
```

`monitor.DB()` calls `migrate(db, MigrateOpts{})` exactly as today — implicit, auto-backup, no rebuilds. Explicit `endless db migrate` accepts flags.

**CLI surface:**
- `endless-event migrate-db [--dry-run] [--force-rebuild] [--target N]` — Go subcommand. JSON output: `{"applied": [{"version": 5, "name": "..."}], "skipped": [...]}`.
- `endless db migrate [--dry-run] [--force-rebuild]` — Python verb shelling out (mirrors event_bridge / query_bridge pattern).

## Implementation phases

Single-session work; one branch / worktree.

**Phase 1 — Registry refactor + version gate**
- Convert `internal/monitor/db.go:migrate` to the registry pattern shown above.
- Add `userVersion(db)` / `setUserVersion(db, n)` helpers.
- Each `migrateVN` function stays as-is (V1-V4 already body-idempotent via IF NOT EXISTS / hasColumn / hasTable). The gate just stops them from re-running unnecessarily.
- One-time backfill: existing DBs have `user_version=0` but already-applied schema. On first run: detect post-V4 schema (e.g. `hasTable("suggestions")`) → fast-path set `user_version=4` and skip. Or: re-run V1-V4 (all idempotent) and let them no-op. Recommend the fast-path so we don't re-trigger backups on every existing install.

**Phase 2 — `endless-event migrate-db` Go subcommand**
- Adds new case to the `endless-event` switch.
- Flags: `--dry-run`, `--force-rebuild`, `--target N`.
- Always calls `migrate(db, opts)`; reports JSON.

**Phase 3 — `endless db migrate` Python verb**
- New `@db_cmd.command("migrate")` in `src/endless/cli.py` (or under `task_cmd` if `db` group doesn't exist yet).
- Shells to `endless-event migrate-db`.
- Surfaces output. Exits non-zero on failure.

**Phase 4 — Document the migration-author contract**
- Short docstring at the top of `db.go:migrations` describing:
  - Increment `CurrentSchemaVersion`.
  - Append to `migrations` slice in order.
  - Body must be idempotent or fail loudly on partial state.
  - Set `RequiresRebuild=true` if any step rebuilds a whole table; user must opt in.
  - No assumptions about prior runs — gate is at framework level.

## Files to create / modify

**Modify:**
- `internal/monitor/db.go` — registry refactor, version helpers, post-V4 detection
- `cmd/endless-event/main.go` — add `migrate-db` case + runner
- `src/endless/cli.py` — add `db migrate` verb (create `db` group if absent)

**Create:**
- (Optional) `src/endless/db_bridge.py` — if you want to keep migration-shellouts in their own module rather than extending `event_bridge.py`. Default: extend `event_bridge.py` to keep the pattern flat.

## Reusable existing helpers
- `internal/monitor/db.go:BackupDB` — existing 60-second-rate-limited backup. Already integrated into `migrate()`; keep.
- `internal/monitor/db.go:hasTable`, `hasColumn` — for post-V4 detection.
- `src/endless/event_bridge.py:emit_event` pattern — shellout/JSON-parse template for the new bridge function.

## Verification

- **Fresh DB**: user_version=0, schema empty → V1-V4 run → user_version=4.
- **Existing post-V4 DB** (typical install today): user_version=0, schema post-V4 → fast-path sets user_version=4 with no migration run, no backup taken.
- **Stability check** (the bug E-1118 just hit): run `endless task show E-X` 10 times — `user_version` and `task_deps` rows unchanged.
- **Hypothetical V5**: bump `CurrentSchemaVersion` to 5, add `migrateV5` to slice. Run `endless db migrate` → V5 runs, version=5.
- **Rebuild gate**: V5 with `RequiresRebuild=true` blocks auto-migrate; only runs with `endless db migrate --force-rebuild`.
- **Dry run**: reports pending migrations without applying.

## Out of scope

- **Port Python's V5+V6 to Go** — E-894 Phase 3. First user of this framework.
- **Remove Python's `_migrate` entirely** — E-894 Phase 5. Until then, Python's `_migrate` keeps running but is now a no-op against a current DB (V1-V4 guards already idempotent; V5 gated by E-1118 patch; V6 idempotent via `hasColumn`).
- **`_schema_version` table with audit history** — pragma user_version is sufficient for our scale.
- **Rebuild policy for V1-V4** — none of them rebuild whole tables. Constraint applies forward.

## Open question for Mike

Two questions worth your call before I implement:

1. **Pragma vs `_schema_version` table** — your original task description said table. I'm recommending pragma. OK to go pragma, or do you want the table?
2. **Python's `_migrate` after E-863 lands** — leave it running (no-op against a current DB) until E-894 Phase 5 deletes it, or short-circuit it now (`if user_version >= 6: return`)? Either works; short-circuit is safer.

payload.title: Add UserPromptSubmit hook with Layer 1 reminder + Layer 2 pivot gate
payload.description: Layer E of E-971: behavioral gate. New UserPromptSubmit handler in cmd/endless-hook/claude.go injects a per-message active-task reminder (Layer 1) and a deterministic gate matching against E-970's kind='pivot' matchers (Layer 2). PreToolUse extended to also block Write/Edit when gate is pending. Gate cleared by 'endless task start/confirm/add' verbs. See attached prompt for full scope and acceptance criteria; design in .endless/plans/E-971.md (Behavioral gate section).

fields.prompt:
# E-971 Layer E — Behavioral gate (UserPromptSubmit hook)

You are picking up the behavioral gate slice of E-971 (parallel-session support via git worktrees in endless). Read `.endless/plans/E-971.md` for full context — particularly the "Behavioral gate" section. This prompt is the short brief.

## Context: what is already shipped

- **Foundation slice**: `endless worktree {list, current, show, for-task}` (read-only).
- **Mutation slice**: `endless worktree {land, drop}` with E-987's auto-commit + rebase + ff-merge + retry.
- **Layer D (just shipped, E-1084)**: SessionStart claim/adopt/create, PreToolUse worktree↔task match, SessionEnd lock release. Worktrees are now lifecycle-managed.
- **E-970**: matchers in config files. Includes `kind='pivot'` rows (case-insensitive substrings like "actually", "wait", "by the way" plus the case-sensitive "PIVOT" override). Read via `matchers.get_pivot_matchers()` in Python or the equivalent Go-side load in `internal/matchers/matchers.go`.
- **E-1012**: PreToolUse hook blocks Claude bash `git commit` on main checkout.

## Your scope: Layer E — UserPromptSubmit hook with two layers

Add a new event handler in `cmd/endless-hook/claude.go` for `UserPromptSubmit`. The event currently fires on every user message but isn't wired for our use; this slice gives it two jobs.

**Strictly this slice.** Do NOT also do Layer F (task-command integration that creates worktrees on `task start`). That will be spawned separately.

### Layer 1 — always-on context refresh

Every UserPromptSubmit event:
- Look up the session's active task via `monitor.GetActiveSession(payload.SessionID)`.
- If there's an active task, inject a system reminder into Claude's context: `"Active task: E-XXX — <title>."` (one line).
- If no active task, inject nothing for Layer 1 (Layer 2 may still fire).
- The existing `hookResponse{AdditionalContext: "..."}` mechanism is the injection vehicle (see how `handleTaskContextInjection` does it).

This is unconditional and cheap. Goal: keep the active task salient at the moment context is most likely to drift.

### Layer 2 — deterministic pivot gate

On every UserPromptSubmit, read the user's prompt text from the payload (the field name in the Claude Code hook payload — verify, but historically it's something like `prompt` in the tool_input or a top-level `user_prompt`/`message` field; check what's actually populated for UserPromptSubmit by inspecting recent payloads in the hook log).

For each enabled matcher of `kind='pivot'`:
- If the matcher is `case_sensitive=true`, search the prompt for an exact case-sensitive substring match (this catches the deliberate `PIVOT` override).
- Otherwise search case-insensitively.

If any pivot matcher matches:
- Block subsequent Write/Edit tools by recording a "gate-pending" state on the session.
- Inject a system reminder: *"Pivot trigger detected (matched: <phrase>). Confirm or update active task before any Write/Edit. Cleared by `endless task add ...` (new task), `endless task start <id>` (continue/switch), or `endless task confirm <id>` (continue same)."*
- The block lives until cleared by one of the listed CLI verbs.

### Gate clearance

The `task` command verbs that clear the gate:
- `endless task start <id>` — already exists; clearing happens via the existing PostToolUse `action`-kind regex match for `task-start` in `claude.go` plus a new gate-clearance action when StartWorkSession runs.
- `endless task confirm <id>` — already exists similarly.
- `endless task add ...` — already exists; on completion, clear the gate.

Implementation hint: gate-pending state is per-session. Add a column or flag to `sessions` table (or a separate small `session_gates` table) — your call. If using sessions table, the migration is small; if separate, it's a clean separation.

### Storage of gate state

Two options:
- **Option A**: New column `sessions.gate_pending_until INTEGER` (timestamp or NULL). PreToolUse checks. Clears via task verbs.
- **Option B**: New small table `session_gates(session_id PRIMARY KEY, triggered_at TEXT, matcher_phrase TEXT)`. PreToolUse joins.

Pick whichever feels cleanest. Option A is simpler if we never need history of gate triggers; Option B is easier to extend later.

### PreToolUse extension (small)

The existing PreToolUse handler from Layer D blocks Write/Edit when worktree↔task doesn't match. Add: if session has a gate-pending state, also block with the gate's message — even if worktree↔task otherwise matches.

## Acceptance criteria

- User prompts with no pivot triggers: Layer 1 reminder appears in Claude's context if there's an active task; nothing else changes.
- User prompts containing "actually" (case-insensitive): pivot detected, Claude is blocked from Write/Edit until cleared.
- User prompts containing "PIVOT" (case-sensitive deliberate): pivot detected, blocked.
- User prompts containing "Actually" (capital-A) when only the case-insensitive matcher exists: pivot detected, blocked.
- User prompts containing "actually" with the case-INSENSITIVE matcher disabled: NOT detected.
- After block fires: `endless task start E-XXX` clears the gate. Subsequent Write succeeds.
- After block fires: `endless task add "Add foo bar" --type chore --phase later` clears the gate.
- After block fires: `endless task confirm E-XXX` clears the gate.
- Disable the only pivot matcher (`endless phrase disable pivot actually`): the trigger no longer fires for that phrase.

## Constraints and conventions

- **Companion file** terminology, not "sidecar".
- **Naming**: snake_case for code/DB identifiers, kebab-case for user-facing strings, Go uses PascalCase/camelCase.
- **Discipline**: do this work in a worktree. Layer D is now live, so SessionStart will create one for you if you start in main. Ship via `endless worktree land <task-id>` when done.
- **Use just install** from main only to refresh the toolchain after landing.
- **Don't run `just install` from your worktree**; use `PYTHONPATH=<worktree>/src endless ...` to test in-progress changes.

## Plan file pointer

`.endless/plans/E-971.md`, particularly the "Behavioral gate (UserPromptSubmit hook)" section.

## When done

Mark the task `verify` (Mike confirms). Use `endless worktree land <task-id>` to ship.

This task's prompt was inadvertently overwritten on 2026-05-02 during E-971's spawn workflow (a Layer E behavioral-gate prompt was mis-attached here, then removed). The task's actual scope is in the description above and concerns adding a per-project worktree-creation extension hook for build/dep glue (analogous to .endless/extensions/use.sh from E-1014). See E-996, E-1014, and E-1085 for related context.
